ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for the complete Pollo alla Diavola dish. FOODON_03311434 (chicken thigh food product) is used as the closest parent representing the primary protein component. The full dish includes harissa paste, Italian herbs, and potato wedges.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (chicken meat, grilled) is the closest defensible parent. The full dish includes spicy chilli paste (harissa), Italian herbs, and potato wedges which are not captured by this code alone.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps to this specific Italian grilled chicken preparation. SNOMED CT does not maintain granular dish-level food codes for this cuisine.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of Pollo alla Diavola consists of one grilled free-range chicken thigh with potato wedges, weighing approximately 380 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, thigh, meat only, cooked, roasted (USDA SR Legacy 05170) combined with component estimation for harissa paste and potato wedges
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central (SR Legacy)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Primary macronutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken thigh meat (FDC ID 171477). The dish also includes potato wedges (estimated ~120 g, contributing approximately 17 g carbohydrate per 100 g of total dish) and harissa paste (~15 g, contributing negligible macros at serving level). A weighted composite per-100 g value has been calculated for the full plated dish (chicken thigh ~245 g + potato wedges ~120 g + harissa ~15 g = 380 g total). Chicken thigh cooked: energy 209 kcal, protein 25.9 g, fat 10.9 g, carb 0 g per 100 g. Potato wedges baked: energy 136 kcal, protein 2.9 g, fat 4.5 g, carb 20.1 g per 100 g (USDA SR Legacy FDC 170030). Composite per 100 g of 380 g dish: energy ~184 kcal, protein ~19.5 g, fat ~9.0 g, carb ~7.0 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — potato (boiled/baked) and mixed meat-potato meal analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Pollo alla Diavola as a composite dish. The GI of the dish is dominated by the potato wedge component. Baked/roasted potato has a GI of approximately 60–85 depending on preparation; however, potato wedges with skin, roasted with oil, have a lower GI (~50–55) due to fat content slowing gastric emptying. Grilled chicken contributes negligible glycemic load. A composite dish GI of 50 is used, consistent with fat-moderated potato preparations reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (50 × 26.6) / 100 = 13.3, where carbohydrate per serving = 7.0 g/100 g × 380 g = 26.6 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from harissa paste and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the harissa paste and seasoning in this dish contribute approximately 280 mg sodium per 100 g (~1064 mg per full serving), which represents a significant portion of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; portion control and requesting reduced-salt preparation are advisable.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from potato wedges
type2_diabetes.guidance: The potato wedge component contributes a moderate glycemic load of approximately 13.3 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of the potato component and pair this dish with non-starchy vegetables to moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Lean grilled protein with moderate saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Pollo alla Diavola is a grilled preparation using free-range chicken thigh, providing approximately 2.4 g saturated fat per 100 g; the use of olive oil and absence of cream or butter makes this a relatively heart-friendly choice compared to fried or cream-based chicken dishes, though total fat intake should be monitored.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pollo alla Diavola, meaning 'Devil's Chicken' in Italian, is a classic Italian grilled chicken preparation traditionally made by butterflying a whole chicken or using bone-in thigh pieces, marinating them in a fiery mixture of chilli, garlic, olive oil, lemon juice, and Italian herbs, then grilling over high heat to achieve a charred, crispy skin with juicy interior. The name references the intense heat of the spices and the high-temperature cooking method. In this Singapore restaurant version served at Ricciotti - The Riverwalk, the dish uses free-range chicken thigh marinated with Italian herbs and harissa spicy chilli pepper paste, grilled and served with potato wedges. The flavour profile is bold, smoky, and spicy with herbaceous notes from rosemary, thyme, and oregano. The texture contrasts crispy charred skin with tender, moist chicken meat. Nutritionally, the dish is a high-protein, moderate-fat meal providing approximately 184 kcal per 100 g, with 19.5 g protein and 9.0 g fat per 100 g. The potato wedges add a moderate carbohydrate component with a glycemic load of approximately 13.3 per full serving. The dish is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free. Clinically, the sodium content from harissa and seasoning warrants attention for hypertensive individuals, while the moderate glycemic load requires portion awareness for those managing Type 2 Diabetes. The grilled preparation method and use of olive oil make it a relatively cardiovascular-friendly choice within Italian restaurant cuisine.

confidence_reason: No exact nutritional entry exists for Pollo alla Diavola as a composite restaurant dish in any approved primary source. Values are derived from a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked chicken thigh and baked potato wedges, adjusted for the harissa and herb components. The GI is an analogous estimate based on fat-moderated potato preparations from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level USDA data is high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Food group corrected from input value 'Grains and staples' to 'Meat and alternatives' as the primary component is grilled chicken thigh. 2. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA SR Legacy FDC 171477 (chicken thigh, cooked, roasted) and USDA SR Legacy FDC 170030 (potato, baked, flesh and skin) weighted by approximate serving proportions (chicken ~245 g, potato ~120 g, harissa ~15 g, total ~380 g). 3. Sodium estimate of 280 mg/100 g accounts for harissa paste sodium contribution (~400 mg/100 g harissa) and natural chicken sodium; actual restaurant sodium may vary. 4. GI of 50 is an analogous estimate for fat-moderated roasted potato wedges per University of Sydney GI Database conventions; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists. 5. Halal status is not confirmed for this specific restaurant preparation; the flag is set to false pending verification. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are not directly reported for this composite dish. 7. This is an Italian cuisine dish; multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. No mandatory non-English local names are required for Italian cuisine under PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 7.2.
